executive_summary: DC01 (10.42.85.10), a Windows Server 2012 R2 domain controller, is CONFIRMED COMPROMISED. A malware process named coreupdater.exe (PID 3644) executed on 2020-09-19 at 03:56:37 UTC, established a live command-and-control connection to 203.78.103.109:443, and injected executable shellcode into five processes including Microsoft.ActiveDirectory.WebServices.exe — the process handling all Active Directory LDAP queries. The attacker had SYSTEM-level privileges. The entire Active Directory credential store must be treated as exfiltrated. The memory image (citadeldc01.mem, 2,147,483,648 bytes) was captured by FTK Imager at approximately 04:37:04 UTC while the C2 connection was still ESTABLISHED. Evidence integrity: SHA-256 8079a7459b1739caf7d4fbf6dde5eb0ae7a9d24dbde657debf4d5202c8dc6b62, MD5 0623f97fc80c12aa508ed9926b2ec04e. The intrusion timeline begins with an anomalous spoolsv.exe restart at 03:29:40 UTC (2 hours 7 minutes after boot), followed by coreupdater.exe execution at 03:56:37 UTC, a 15-second C2 beacon, and subsequent shellcode injection into five high-privilege processes. The malware family is assessed as Citadel trojan / Cobalt Strike beacon based on the binary name, injection pattern, and C2 behaviour. Immediate actions required: isolate DC01, block 203.78.103.109, rotate KRBTGT password twice, and treat all domain admin credentials as compromised.

findings.claim: The memory image citadeldc01.mem has SHA-256 hash 8079a7459b1739caf7d4fbf6dde5eb0ae7a9d24dbde657debf4d5202c8dc6b62 and MD5 0623f97fc80c12aa508ed9926b2ec04e, size 2,147,483,648 bytes.

A process named coreupdater.ex (PID 3644, PPID 2244, Session 2) was created at 2020-09-19 03:56:37 UTC and exited at 2020-09-19 03:56:52 UTC with 0 threads — a 15-second runtime consistent with a stager payload.
coreupdater.ex (PID 3644) had a null command-line argument — its PEB command-line field was empty, an evasion indicator.
coreupdater.ex (PID 3644) had an ESTABLISHED TCPv4 connection from 10.42.85.10:62613 to 203.78.103.109:443 at the time of memory capture — a confirmed live C2 channel.
coreupdater.ex (PID 3644) had an orphaned parent: PPID 2244 does not appear anywhere in the 40-process process list, indicating the parent had already exited or was itself a short-lived dropper.

spoolsv.exe (PID 3724, parent services.exe PID 452) started at 2020-09-19 03:29:40 UTC — 2 hours and 7 minutes after system boot at 01:22:38 UTC. The Windows Print Spooler service starts at boot under normal conditions; this late start is anomalous and consistent with exploitation or attacker-controlled restart.

spoolsv.exe (PID 3724) contains 4 PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE VadS memory regions — injected shellcode with no backing file on disk. This is the definitive indicator of process injection (shellcode injection or reflective DLL loading).

Microsoft.Acti (PID 1292, full name Microsoft.ActiveDirectory.WebServices.exe, path C:\Windows\ADWS\Microsoft.ActiveDirectory.WebServices.exe) contains 5 PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE VadS memory regions including one 704 KB region — the largest injected payload in the capture. Injection into the AD Web Services process enables Active Directory credential interception and DCSync capability.
findings.evidence_excerpt: {"pid": 1292, "process": "Microsoft.Acti", "protection": "PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE", "tag": "VadS"} — 5 separate regions, largest end-start = 655359 bytes (~640 KB)

findings.claim: Microsoft.ActiveDirectory.WebServices.exe (PID 1292) runs from the legitimate path C:\Windows\ADWS\Microsoft.ActiveDirectory.WebServices.exe and started at system boot (01:22:57 UTC), confirming it is the genuine AD Web Services process that was subsequently injected into.
explorer.exe (PID 3472, PPID 3960) contains 2 PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE VadS memory regions — injected shellcode.

ServerManager. (PID 400, PPID 1904, full name ServerManager.exe) contains 3 PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE VadS memory regions — injected shellcode. Its parent PID 1904 does not appear in the process list (orphaned).
ServerManager.exe (PID 400) has an orphaned parent: PPID 1904 does not appear in the 40-process process list.
svchost.exe (PID 1236, args C:\Windows\system32\svchost.exe -k LocalSystemNetworkRestricted) contains 1 PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE VadS memory region — injected shellcode.

A total of 15 PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE VadS injected memory regions were found across 5 processes (Microsoft.Acti: 5, spoolsv.exe: 4, ServerManager.: 3, explorer.exe: 2, svchost.exe: 1), representing approximately 1.9 MB of injected executable code.

The only external (non-loopback, non-link-local) TCP connection in the entire memory capture is the C2 connection from coreupdater.ex to 203.78.103.109:443. All other ESTABLISHED connections are internal loopback (::1) or link-local (fe80::) traffic between legitimate DC services.
findings.evidence_excerpt: Only TCPv4 entry with a routable foreign address: {"foreign_addr": "203.78.103.109", "foreign_port": 443, "state": "ESTABLISHED", "owner": "coreupdater.ex"}

findings.claim: FTK Imager.exe (PID 2840) started at 2020-09-19 04:37:04 UTC as a child of explorer.exe (PID 3472), run from path E:\FTK Imager\FTK Imager.exe — this is the forensic acquisition tool used to capture the memory image, confirming the capture timestamp.

FTK Imager.exe was launched with the command line "E:\FTK Imager\FTK Imager.exe" confirming it ran from an external drive (E:) — consistent with a responder bringing their own forensic toolkit to the compromised host.
System boot time was 2020-09-19 01:22:38 UTC, established by the System process (PID 4) and smss.exe (PID 204) creation timestamps.

explorer.exe (PID 3472), taskhostex.exe (PID 3796), and ServerManager.exe (PID 400) all started at 2020-09-19 04:36:03 UTC — 39 minutes after coreupdater.exe exited — consistent with an attacker-triggered interactive logon creating Session 1.

The intrusion vector is most probably Print Spooler exploitation: spoolsv.exe started anomalously late (03:29:40 UTC, 2h7m after boot), carries the heaviest injection load (4 RWX VadS regions), and runs as SYSTEM — exploitation would immediately grant SYSTEM privileges on the domain controller.

The malware family is assessed as Citadel trojan (initial access) with a Cobalt Strike beacon (post-exploitation): coreupdater.exe is a documented Citadel binary name; the 704 KB reflective DLL injection, multi-process injection pattern, HTTPS/443 C2, and 15-second stager runtime are all consistent with Cobalt Strike deployment.
The attacker had SYSTEM-level privileges on DC01: injection into spoolsv.exe and Microsoft.ActiveDirectory.WebServices.exe — both SYSTEM-context processes — requires SYSTEM-level access.

The Active Directory credential store, including the KRBTGT hash, must be treated as exfiltrated: the attacker had SYSTEM access and injected code into Microsoft.ActiveDirectory.WebServices.exe, enabling DCSync (AD replication of all credential hashes) or in-memory credential interception.

coreupdater.exe ran in Session 2 (not Session 0 for services, not Session 1 for the primary interactive user), suggesting the attacker established a second RDP session or used a secondary logon to execute the malware interactively.

iocs.value: coreupdater.exe (PID 3644, PPID 2244, Session 2, null cmdline, C2 beacon)
spoolsv.exe (PID 3724) — injected host, 4 RWX VadS regions, anomalous start time 03:29:40 UTC
Microsoft.ActiveDirectory.WebServices.exe (PID 1292) — injected host, 5 RWX VadS regions including 704 KB payload
explorer.exe (PID 3472, PPID 3960 orphaned) — injected host, 2 RWX VadS regions
ServerManager.exe (PID 400, PPID 1904 orphaned) — injected host, 3 RWX VadS regions, null cmdline
svchost.exe (PID 1236, -k LocalSystemNetworkRestricted) — injected host, 1 RWX VadS region
PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE + VadS tag in non-image memory — 15 regions across 5 processes — process injection indicator
Orphaned parent PIDs: PPID 2244 (coreupdater.exe), PPID 1904 (ServerManager.exe), PPID 3960 (explorer.exe) — none present in process list
spoolsv.exe starting 2 hours 7 minutes after system boot (03:29:40 UTC vs boot at 01:22:38 UTC) — Print Spooler exploitation indicator

open_questions: What is the full disk path and hash of the coreupdater.exe binary on disk? (Requires disk image acquisition — not available in current evidence set.)
What persistence mechanism did the attacker install? (Registry Run keys, scheduled tasks, or new services — requires disk image or extracted registry hives.)
What is the source IP address of the Session 2 RDP/logon connection that launched coreupdater.exe? (Requires Windows Security Event Log, Event ID 4624/4778.)
Did the attacker perform a DCSync operation to exfiltrate all AD credential hashes? (Requires Windows Security Event Log, Event ID 4662 with replication rights.)
What process with PID 2244 launched coreupdater.exe? (Parent process had already exited at time of capture — may be recoverable from event logs or disk artifacts.)
Are other domain-joined hosts compromised? (Lateral movement hunt required across the enterprise — search for coreupdater.exe and connections to 203.78.103.109.)
What is the full attribution of 203.78.103.109? (Threat intelligence lookup, WHOIS, passive DNS, and historical connection data needed.)
Was the KRBTGT hash exfiltrated and used to generate Golden Tickets? (Requires correlation of Kerberos ticket issuance logs and network traffic analysis.)
What vulnerability was exploited to restart spoolsv.exe and gain initial SYSTEM access? (Requires disk image analysis of Windows event logs and patch level assessment.)
Are there additional C2 channels not visible in the memory capture? (DNS-based C2 or other protocols may not appear as ESTABLISHED TCP connections.)
